Hormuz naval mines would represent a severe new escalation — they are difficult to detect and clear quickly, would force complete suspension of commercial traffic, and could trigger direct US Navy mine-sweeping operations. The Trump post functions simultaneously as a public ultimatum, a deterrent signal, and — given the CBS intel report — likely a pre-justification for forthcoming military action against Iranian naval assets or mine-laying craft.

item.title: Russia: Isfahan Consulate Damaged in Strikes — Zakharova Calls It 'Flagrant Violation' of International Law
item.description: Russia's Foreign Ministry confirmed its consulate in Isfahan, Iran was damaged over the weekend when strikes hit the nearby local governor's office. Spokesperson Maria Zakharova called the incident a 'flagrant violation of international law' but did not directly accuse any country of responsibility. No casualties or serious injuries were reported. Zakharova said: 'The conflict in the Middle East continues to escalate, directly or indirectly affecting the interests of an increasing number of states and their civilians.' The incident carries acute diplomatic weight given Russia's contested role in the war — Washington Post reported Russia is providing real-time targeting intelligence on US assets to Iran, a claim Araghchi confirmed on NBC but which Putin denied to Trump in their March 9 phone call. Hitting a Russian diplomatic facility — even collaterally — raises the stakes of Moscow's involvement and gives Zakharova a public grievance platform without escalating to a direct accusation.

item.title: Iran Strikes UAE's Ruwais Refinery — Middle East's Largest, 922K bpd — 9 Drones Penetrate Air Defences
item.description: Iran launched a drone strike on the UAE's Ruwais Industrial Complex on Tuesday evening, home to the biggest refinery in the Middle East. The Abu Dhabi Media Office confirmed a fire broke out at the ADNOC-operated site, with no injuries reported. Reuters and AFP sources said the refinery halted operations as a precaution. ADNOC's Ruwais complex produces 922,000 barrels of oil per day — making this the largest single energy infrastructure target struck in the UAE so far. The UAE Ministry of Defence said 8 ballistic missiles and 26 drones were intercepted on Tuesday, but 9 drones got through air defences. In response to the surge in overnight attacks, the UAE has changed its national early warning system: mobile phone alerts will now be a quiet ping rather than the loud repeated beep used earlier in the conflict, to avoid waking residents unnecessarily during overnight salvos.

item.title: Pentagon: ~140 US Service Members Wounded Since War Began — 8 Remain Severely Injured
item.description: Pentagon spokesperson Sean Parnell released an official casualty statement Tuesday: approximately 140 US service members have been wounded since Operation Epic Fury began on February 28. "The vast majority of these injuries have been minor," Parnell said, with 108 service members already returned to duty. Eight service members remain listed as severely injured and are receiving the highest level of medical care. The US has previously confirmed 8 killed in action — bringing the total combat casualty figure to approximately 148 US military personnel. The first 6 KIA were Army reservists killed in a drone strike at a Kuwaiti port on March 1; the 7th was Sgt. Benjamin Pennington, who died March 8 from injuries sustained at Prince Sultan Air Base; an 8th KIA was confirmed on Day 11.

item.title: Leavitt: War Ends With Iran's "Complete Unconditional Surrender" — Navy Has NOT Escorted Tanker Through Hormuz
item.description: White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt said at Tuesday's briefing that the initial US timeline was four to six weeks to destroy Iran's missiles and navy, eliminate its nuclear capabilities, and wipe out its proxies — but the operation is ahead of schedule. The war will not end until Iran's "complete and unconditional surrender, whether they say it or not," Leavitt said, adding that Trump will personally determine when Iran no longer poses a direct threat. Leavitt also explicitly contradicted US Energy Secretary Chris Wright's now-deleted tweet, clarifying that the US Navy has NOT escorted an oil tanker through the Strait of Hormuz. Wright's post had claimed "the US Navy successfully escorted an oil tanker through the Strait of Hormuz to ensure oil remains flowing to global markets" — the claim briefly sent oil prices from $94 to $82 a barrel before Wright deleted it. Leavitt's denial restores the baseline: Hormuz remains contested, and the Navy is still drawing up "additional options" per Trump's directive.

item.title: White House Briefing: Iran Missiles Down 90%, 50+ Ships Destroyed — Trump "Not Afraid" to Use New Hormuz Options
item.description: White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt delivered the official US battle damage assessment at the daily briefing (18:29-18:33 GMT, Day 12). Key lines: Iran's ballistic missile attacks are down more than 90% and drone attacks down ~85% since Operation Epic Fury began. The US has destroyed more than 50 Iranian naval vessels. President Trump is "committed to allowing the free flow of oil through the Strait of Hormuz," and the US military is drawing up "additional options" following Trump's directive — options Leavitt declined to specify, saying only "the president is not afraid to use them." On energy: Leavitt called the current price surge "temporary," saying Americans will see oil and gas prices "drop rapidly" once military objectives are "fully achieved." She framed the operation as removing "terrorists who indiscriminately target civilians and attempt to hold the global economy hostage." No ceasefire timeline given. The briefing came as Iran fired a fresh missile salvo at Israel (18:02 GMT) and oil had already dropped to $82/barrel earlier in the session on Hormuz escort signals. (Day 12, ~18:33 GMT)

item.title: BREAKING: Iran Launches Fresh Missile Salvo at Israel — IDF Intercepts Active, Alerts Sent Nationwide
item.description: The Israeli Defense Forces report a new wave of Iranian missiles launched at Israel's borders, with interception operations underway. The IDF posted on social media: "Upon receiving an alert, the public is instructed to enter a protected space and remain there until further notice." Mobile phone alerts were sent to affected areas across Israel. The new launch comes on the same day US Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth declared it would be the "most intense" day of US strikes on Iran — with "the most fighters, the most bombers, the most strikes." Iran's retaliatory fire continues despite Hegseth claiming Iran had fired the fewest missiles in a 24-hour period since the war began. Meanwhile, White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt's daily briefing is imminent. Oil had dropped to $82/barrel earlier on ceasefire signals — new launches may reverse that trend. (Day 12, BREAKING ~18:02 GMT)

item.title: Oil Drops to $82 as US Energy Secretary's Deleted Tweet Claims Navy Escorted Tanker Through Hormuz
item.description: Brent crude plunged from $94 to $82 per barrel at 17:30 GMT Tuesday after US Energy Secretary Chris Wright posted — and then deleted — a claim that the US Navy had successfully escorted an oil tanker through the Strait of Hormuz. Wright's now-deleted X post read: "President Trump is maintaining stability of global energy during the military operations against Iran. The U.S. Navy successfully escorted an oil tanker through the Strait of Hormuz to ensure oil remains flowing to global markets." The post has since disappeared, but its market impact was immediate and sharp — a ~$12 drop in under an hour. Oil remains well above pre-war levels (~$72/barrel). The deletion raises questions about whether the escort was premature to announce or politically sensitive. BBC Business News editor Mark Broad confirmed the content and timeline. This is the first public statement — even if retracted — of an active US Navy escort mission through the Hormuz blockade zone, a development the market treated as a major ceasefire signal. (Day 12, ~17:49 GMT)

item.title: BREAKING: Fresh Explosions Hit Tehran — BBC Persian and AFP Confirm New Wave of Strikes
item.description: More explosions have been heard in Tehran, with BBC Persian colleagues reporting the Iranian capital is currently being targeted. Journalists from Agence France-Presse (AFP) confirmed hearing three explosions in the city. The BBC live blog headline was simultaneously updated to lead with "Explosions in Tehran" — a BBC editorial signal of a new active strike wave. This comes as US Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth had earlier declared today would be "the most intense day" of US strikes on Iran so far, with "the most fighters, the most bombers, the most strikes." The new explosions add to an already intense Day 12 of the conflict.
item.source: BBC News Live / BBC Persian / AFP

item.title: Iranian Red Crescent: 19,000+ Civilian Units Damaged — 77 Medical Centres, 69 Schools Among Casualties
item.description: Iran's Red Crescent Society reports more than 19,000 civilian "units" have been damaged since the war began: 16,000+ residential and 3,000+ commercial. Among specifically damaged facilities: 77 pharmaceutical and medical centres, and 69 schools. Data sourced via US-based Human Rights Activists News Agency (HRANA). The figures emerged as US Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth separately said the US would investigate allegations of civilian casualties in Iran, adding: "No nation takes more precautions to ensure there's never targeting of civilians than the United States of America." The Red Crescent damage count represents the most comprehensive tally of civilian infrastructure destruction yet reported for the 12-day conflict.
item.source: BBC News Live / Iranian Red Crescent Society via HRANA

item.title: Three US B-1 Bombers Depart RAF Fairford as UK Denies Involvement in Iran Strikes
item.description:
BBC reporter Ellie Price, on the ground at RAF Fairford in south-west England, reports three B-1 bombers took off while she watched. Plane-spotters and photographers at the base counted 12 B-1 bombers total after four more landed earlier in the day. Engines were running on additional aircraft and personnel were carrying out checks. Neither the UK Ministry of Defence nor the Pentagon has confirmed whether any of these aircraft have been involved in bombing missions against Iran. The deployment of US strategic bombers to UK sovereign territory — and their active departure — represents direct observable eviden
[Result truncated from 430355 to 100000 characters]
